form_title: English Language Proficiency Assessment V01 '21 WMS Pre-Requisite
reading_title: DEEPWATER HORIZON OIL SPILL – BP GULF OF MEXICO OIL SPILL
reading_passage:
On 20 April 2010, the offshore drilling rig Deepwater Horizon exploded and caught fire in US waters of the Gulf of Mexico.  11 workers were killed in the explosion and 17 injured.  More than 110 worker escaped from the rig. The rig was owned by Transocean. It was on lease to BP, which was the main operator and developer of the site, with Anadarko Petroleum and MOEX Offshore (part of Mitsui Oil Exploration) as minority co-owners.

The Deepwater Horizon disaster released approximately 134 million gallons (3.19 million barrels) of oil into the environment. Every day for 87 days, BP’s Macondo well released an average of more than 1.5 million gallons of fresh oil into the ocean—the equivalent of a large oil spill occurring every day for nearly 3 months.

The “blowout preventer” was built by Cameron International.  On 22 April, the rig sank.  Oil leaked from the ruptured well head until 15 July when it was temporarily stopped; approximately 4.9 million barrels of oil had leaked into the Gulf.  Oil directly affected coastal areas in Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, Florida and Texas.

The Gulf of Mexico commercial fishing industry was estimated to have lost $247 million as a result of post spill fisheries closures. The explosion and spill have given rise to many lawsuits.

By early December 2010, hundreds of lawsuits had been filed against the companies involved; this number is likely to increase further.  People claiming to have suffered economic harm from the spill can also seek compensation through the Gulf Coast Claims Facility.  Emergency payments do not prevent the claimants from suing for additional or future losses; however, if a long-term settlement offer is accepted and received, the claimant forgoes the right to sue BP.

The oil released into the environment by the Deepwater Horizon incident was toxic to a wide range of organisms. More than 8,000 birds, sea turtles, and marine mammals were found injured or dead in the six months after the spill.

Deepwater Horizon was an ultra-deepwater, dynamically positioned, semi-submersible offshore oil drilling rig owned by Transocean.

Five years after oil from the Deepwater Horizon disaster tainted the Panhandle's sugar-white beaches, petroleum giant BP agreed to an $18.7 billion settlement with Florida, four other Gulf States and the federal government. BP Oil Spill Settlement Amount Finalized, Rises to More Than $20B.
